question_data.text: According to Sir Edward Coke, land in its legal significance comprehends:
options: Only the ground and soil.
Only immoveable property attached to the earth.
“Any ground, soil or earth whatsoever,” including castles, houses, and other buildings.
Only what is attached to the land and the minerals beneath.
question_data.explanation: Coke's definition is broad, covering both the soil and anything built upon it.

question_data.text: In Nigeria, the definition of land is similar to the common law, but explicitly excludes:
options: All houses and buildings.
All resources that are vested in the federal government by the 1999 Constitution.
All chattel attached to the land.
Airspace above a reasonable height.
question_data.explanation: The Constitution and Land Use Act vest specific resources (minerals, water) in the state.

question_data.text: Trespass to land, also known as Trespass quare clausum fregit, is committed when the defendant, without lawful justification:
options: Indirectly causes a nuisance to the plaintiff's land.
Enters upon land in the possession of the plaintiff, or remains upon such land, or directly places or projects any material object upon such land.
Interferes with the plaintiff's ownership of the land.
Takes chattel from the land.
question_data.explanation: Trespass to land requires direct and unpermitted physical presence or projection.

question_data.text: Trespass to land is fundamentally an injury to or interference with which right?
options: Ownership right.
Reversionary right.
Possessory right or interest in land.
Statutory right of entry.
question_data.explanation: Trespass protects possession, not necessarily ownership/title.

question_data.text: What is the actionability status of Trespass to Land?
options: Actionable only upon proof of substantial damage.
Actionable only if committed intentionally.
Actionable per se without proof of damage.
Actionable only if a court order is first obtained.
question_data.explanation: Like other trespasses, it is actionable 'per se' to protect the inviolability of land.

question_data.text: The primary interest protected by the tort of trespass to land is:
options: Ownership.
Title.
Exclusive Possession.
Statutory Right.
question_data.explanation: The exclusive right to exclude others from one's land is the core protected interest.

question_data.text: Which of the following is NOT an essential element of trespass to land?
options: Any direct interference.
With land.
In the possession of another person.
Proof of substantial damage.
question_data.explanation: Proof of damage is not required for a trespass claim to succeed.

question_data.text: When an interference with land is consequential or indirect, the proper remedy is often in the tort of:
options: Conversion.
Trespass to Chattel.
Nuisance.
Detinue.
question_data.explanation: Nuisance covers indirect interferences like smell, noise, or spreading roots.

question_data.text: In the case of Entick v. Carrington, the principle established was that:
options: Mistake is a valid defence for trespass.
Every invasion of private property, “be it so minute,” is a trespass.
Trespass is only actionable if it causes substantial damage.
An owner without immediate possession can sue for trespass.
question_data.explanation: Lord Camden famously held that any unauthorized entry, however small, is a trespass.

question_data.text: In the case of Basely v. Clarkson, the defendant who innocently mowed his neighbor's lawn by mistake was held liable for trespass. This illustrates that:
options: The lack of motive vitiates an action for trespass.
Mistake is a strong defence to trespass.
Mistake is not a defence, and intention is essential in determining trespass.
Only intentional and not unintentional acts are trespass.
question_data.explanation: Intention to enter the area is enough; knowledge that the area belongs to another isn't required.

question_data.text: When a person abuses the purpose for which he has been permitted to be on a land, he becomes a:
options: Licensee ab initio.
Trespasser ab initio.
Co-owner.
Bailee.
question_data.explanation: Abusing a legal authority to enter makes one a trespasser from the beginning (ab initio).

question_data.text: The rule in Harrison v. Duke of Rutland held that the plaintiff, who entered the defendant’s land to interrupt a grouse shooting party, was liable for trespass because:
options: The defendant did not have a statutory right of entry.
The use was outside the lawful purpose of being on the land.
The plaintiff abused his license granted by the owner.
He committed destruction waste of common property.
question_data.explanation: Entry for an unauthorized purpose (interference) turned the presence into a trespass.

question_data.text: Trespass to land may be committed in which of the following ways?
options: Airspace above the land to a reasonable extent for user.
Earth surface or land surface.
Beneath the earth surface.
All of the above.
question_data.explanation: Land includes everything above and below the surface (with reasonable limits).

question_data.text: In Kelsen v. Imperial Tobacco Co. Ltd, the projection of an advertisement signboard into the plaintiff's airspace a few feet was held to be:
options: An inevitable accident.
Not a trespass, as no harm resulted.
A trespass, and the plaintiff was entitled to an injunction for its removal.
A public nuisance.
question_data.explanation: Unauthorized intrusion into usable airspace is a trespass.

question_data.text: In Bernstein v. Skyviews and General Ltd, the plane flying at 630 feet above the plaintiff's land was held not to be trespassing because:
options: It was a statutory right of entry.
The flight was too high to interfere with the reasonable use and enjoyment of the land.
The defendant was an agent of the third party with a better title.
The plaintiff failed to prove de facto possession.
question_data.explanation: Airspace rights are limited to the height necessary for the ordinary use and enjoyment of the land.

question_data.text: For a person to sue for trespass to land, which form of possession is required?
options: Mere physical presence.
De facto (actual) possession.
Possession only on the basis of having legal title.
Constructive possession only.
question_data.explanation: Trespass is primarily an action for the possessor in fact.

question_data.text: According to the key points, which party cannot sue for trespass to land based on mere physical presence or usage of control?
options: An owner of landed property.
A tenant.
A servant in a house.
A holder of reversionary interest (if damage is permanent).
question_data.explanation: Servants/guests have 'custody' or 'use' but not legal 'possession' required for trespass.

question_data.text: Which of the following is an example of trespass to land by wrongful entry?
options: An omission to fill up a trench.
The failure of a tenant to pay his bill.
Walking on or crossing another person's land without lawful justification.
Allowing the roots of a tree to spread from adjacent land.
question_data.explanation: Unauthorized entry by person or object is the most common form of trespass.

question_data.text: In Basely v. Clarkson, the court's finding that the defendant was liable for trespass, despite his mistake, affirmed the principle that:
options: Where there is trespass by mistake, only negligence can be claimed.
Mistake of fact or law is not a defence to trespass to land.
Intentional trespassers are not strictly liable.
Unintentional entry can never be trespass.
question_data.explanation: A person acts 'at their peril' when entering land.

question_data.text: Trespass by Remaining on Another Person's Land occurs when a person:
options: Enters the land with lawful justification.
Stays after his purpose or right of entry has come to an end.
Remains in possession for more than 12 years.
Sues for recovery of possession.
question_data.explanation: Presence that was once lawful becomes a trespass if the license/authority expires or is revoked.

question_data.text: In Balogun v. Alakija, the defendant was held liable for trespass because:
options: He entered the house illegally.
He failed to leave within 15 minutes after the plaintiff requested him to leave.
He did not pay his rent.
He committed a crime of malicious damage.
question_data.explanation: Remaining after a request to leave constitutes a trespass.

question_data.text: According to the document, in McPhil v. Persons Unknown, what did the English Court of Appeal hold regarding squatters?
options: They were entitled to a lengthy notice before eviction.
They were protected by the Residential and Recovery of Premises law.
They were trespassers and the plaintiff was entitled to an order for ejectment.
They had matured their wrongful possession into ownership.
question_data.explanation: Squatters have no legal right to remain and can be evicted as trespassers.

question_data.text: Trespass by Placing Things on the Land is a direct invasion. What form of invasion is allowing the roots of a tree to spread from an adjacent land?
options: Trespass by wrongful entry.
Continuing trespass.
Indirect invasion, which is Nuisance.
Trespass ab initio.
question_data.explanation: Encroachments like roots/branches that require time to grow are indirect and thus nuisance.

question_data.text: What is the doctrine of Trespass Ab Initio?
options: When an entry is accidental, such as a vehicle skidding.
When a person with lawful authority of law abuses their right of entry by misbehaving, making them a trespasser from the moment of entry.
When an owner re-enters the land after a lease is terminated.
When an omission, such as failure to pay a bill, makes a person a trespasser.
question_data.explanation: Authority given by law (not by the party) if abused, results in retroactive trespass.

question_data.text: In the Six Carpenters Case, the carpenters who ate and drank but refused to pay were held NOT to be trespassers ab initio because:
options: An omission cannot make a party a trespasser from the beginning.
The authority to enter was given by the proprietor (the party), not by law.
The amount of damage was nominal.
They had a statutory right of entry.
question_data.explanation: The rule only applies to authority given by law, not by the proprietor.

question_data.text: Mesne profit is a remedy a landlord may claim against a tenant who 'holds over' (overstays). What is mesne profit?
options: Aggravated damages for the initial trespass.
Intermediate profit, money, or compensation payable for the use of the premises until the tenant vacates.
The cost of reinstatement and repairs to the property.
Statutory penalty for failure to give a proper quit notice.
question_data.explanation: Mesne profits are the value of the 'use and occupation' of the land during wrongful possession.

question_data.text: What act constitutes a 'Continuing Trespass'?
options: An act of trespass that has ended, such as digging a trench and leaving.
Remaining on the land or leaving anything on the land after an initial trespass.
A defendant entering to retake a chattel.
A tenant 'holding over' after notice to quit.
question_data.explanation: Leaving an object on someone's land is a new trespass every day until removed.

question_data.text: What is the legal position of a plaintiff who is an original trespasser but is in exclusive de facto possession of the land?
options: They cannot maintain an action against a later trespasser.
They can only sue the true owner.
They can maintain an action in trespass against a later trespasser who cannot show a better title.
They are only entitled to nominal damages.
question_data.explanation: Possession (even wrongful) is good against all except the true owner (beati possidentes).

question_data.text: Under the doctrine of 'trespass by relation,' a person with the right to immediate possession who enters the land is deemed to have been in possession from what point in time?
options: The moment of filing the lawsuit.
The time his right of entry occurred.
The time the trespasser was ejected.
The day the lease expired.
question_data.explanation: The right to possession 'relates back' to when the right first accrued once entry is made.

question_data.text: Which of the following is a valid defence for a defendant in an action for Trespass to Land?
options: Mistake of law or fact.
Absence of motive.
Entry by consent, license, or permission of the occupier.
The fact that the defendant is a servant in the house.
question_data.explanation: Permission (leave and license) is a complete justification for entry.
